content:
The **heart** is a hollow, **muscular organ** that pumps blood throughout the body. It lies in the chest cavity, tilted slightly to the left, enclosed in the **pericardium** (pericardial fluid cushions against shocks).

**Four chambers:**
• **Right atrium (RA)** — receives **deoxygenated** blood from body via **vena cava**.
• **Right ventricle (RV)** — pumps deoxygenated blood to **lungs** via **pulmonary artery**.
• **Left atrium (LA)** — receives **oxygenated** blood from **lungs** via **pulmonary veins**.
• **Left ventricle (LV)** — pumps oxygenated blood to **entire body** via **aorta**; has the **thickest wall** (pumps farthest, highest pressure).

**Other structures:**
• **Septum** — muscular wall dividing heart into right and left halves; prevents mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.
• **Tricuspid valve** — between RA and RV (right side).
• **Bicuspid (mitral) valve** — between LA and LV (left side).
• Valves ensure **one-way flow** — prevent backflow of blood.

**Must know**
• Atria = upper, thin-walled **receiving** chambers.
• Ventricles = lower, thick-walled **pumping** chambers.
• Left side = oxygenated blood; Right side = deoxygenated blood.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Atrium — upper chamber of heart receiving blood from veins.
• Ventricle — lower chamber of heart pumping blood into arteries.
• Septum — partition separating right and left sides of heart.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Atria have thicker walls than ventricles' is FALSE — ventricles are thicker (they pump harder).
• Left ventricle wall is thickest — pumps blood to whole body.
• Tricuspid = right side; Bicuspid/Mitral = left side.
teacherTip: Right-Lung, Left-Body: Right side sends blood to lungs; Left side sends blood to body.
examTip: Label all four chambers + septum + two valves in diagram questions — incomplete labels lose 1–2 marks.

content:
Humans have **double circulation** — blood passes through the heart **twice** during one complete circuit of the body.

**Why double circulation?**
• Blood going to lungs (for oxygen) returns to heart before going to body.
• Allows **oxygenated blood** to be pumped to body at **high pressure** — efficient delivery to all tissues.
• Keeps oxygenated and deoxygenated blood **separate** for maximum efficiency.

**Two phases:**
• **Pulmonary circulation** — heart → lungs → heart (oxygenation).
• **Systemic circulation** — heart → body organs → heart (delivery of O₂ and collection of CO₂).

**Complete cycle summary:**
Body (deoxygenated) → Vena cava → RA → RV → Pulmonary artery → Lungs → Pulmonary veins → LA → LV → Aorta → Body (oxygenated).

**Must know**
• Double circulation = two circuits through heart in one complete journey.
• Advantage: high-pressure systemic circulation for efficient oxygen delivery.
• Open circulation (blood in body cavities) occurs in some invertebrates — humans have **closed** circulation (blood in vessels).
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Double circulation — blood flows through the heart twice in one complete body circuit.
• Closed circulatory system — blood flows entirely within blood vessels.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Blood passes through heart once per circuit' is FALSE for humans.
• Open vs closed: humans = blood in vessels (closed); insects = blood in sinuses (open).
• Double circulation is found in birds and mammals, not all animals.
teacherTip: Double = two trips through the heart: once to lungs, once to body.
examTip: Define double circulation + name both phases (pulmonary + systemic) + state one advantage — standard 3-mark answer.

content:
**Three types of blood vessels:**

**Arteries:**
• Carry blood **away from the heart** to organs.
• Generally carry **oxygenated** blood (exception: **pulmonary artery** carries deoxygenated blood to lungs).
• **Thick, elastic, muscular walls** — withstand high pressure from heart pumping.
• No valves (except near heart).
• **Aorta** — largest artery; branches to supply whole body.

**Veins:**
• Carry blood **towards the heart** from organs.
• Generally carry **deoxygenated** blood (exception: **pulmonary veins** carry oxygenated blood from lungs).
• **Thinner walls**, larger lumen, **valves** prevent backflow of blood.
• Lower pressure than arteries.

**Capillaries:**
• **Finest, thinnest** vessels — connect arteries to veins.
• Walls are **one cell thick** (endothelium only).
• Site of **exchange** — O₂, CO₂, nutrients, wastes, water pass between blood and tissue cells.

**Must know**
• Arteries = Away from heart. Veins = towards heart.
• Pulmonary artery = deoxygenated; Pulmonary vein = oxygenated — **two key exceptions**.
• Exchange happens ONLY in capillaries, not in arteries/veins.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Artery — thick-walled vessel carrying blood away from heart under high pressure.
• Vein — thinner-walled vessel carrying blood toward heart; has valves.
• Capillary — microscopic vessel where exchange of materials occurs between blood and tissues.

**Exam traps:**
• 'All arteries carry oxygenated blood' is FALSE — pulmonary artery is deoxygenated.
• 'Veins carry blood away from heart' is FALSE — arteries do.
• 'None of the arteries carry deoxygenated blood' is FALSE.
teacherTip: A-way = Arteries. Veins come back. Capillaries Connect and exchange.
examTip: Always mention pulmonary artery/vein exceptions when writing the general artery/vein rule — examiners reward this.

content:
**Blood** is a fluid connective tissue. About **55% plasma + 45% formed elements**.

**1. Plasma (liquid part):**
• Straw-coloured fluid; ~90% water.
• Dissolved nutrients, hormones, proteins (fibrinogen, albumin), waste (urea, CO₂), antibodies.
• Transports all dissolved substances.

**2. Red Blood Cells (RBCs / Erythrocytes):**
• Biconcave, **no nucleus** in mature human RBC.
• Contain **haemoglobin** — iron-containing pigment that binds **oxygen**.
• Transport O₂ from lungs to tissues; carry some CO₂ back.
• Made in bone marrow.

**3. White Blood Cells (WBCs / Leucocytes):**
• Have nucleus; fewer in number than RBCs.
• **Fight infection** — phagocytosis, antibody production; provide **immunity**.
• Types: lymphocytes, neutrophils, monocytes, etc.

**4. Platelets (Thrombocytes):**
• Small cell fragments (no nucleus).
• Help in **blood clotting** at wound sites — prevent excessive blood loss.
• Form fibrin mesh to seal injury.

**Must know**
• RBCs carry O₂ (haemoglobin); WBCs fight germs — do NOT swap.
• Blood transports O₂, CO₂, nutrients, hormones AND wastes.
• Lymph lacks RBCs — cannot transport respiratory gases.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Haemoglobin — red pigment in RBCs that combines with oxygen to form oxyhaemoglobin.
• Phagocytosis — process by which WBCs engulf and destroy pathogens.
• Blood clotting — cascade forming fibrin mesh to seal a wound (initiated by platelets).

**Exam traps:**
• 'WBCs carry oxygen' is FALSE.
• 'RBCs fight infection' is FALSE.
• 'Blood does not carry CO₂' is FALSE — blood carries both respiratory gases.
teacherTip: RBC = Red = oxygen Road. WBC = Warriors. Platelets = Patches (plug wounds).
examTip: Name all four components with one function each — a guaranteed 4-mark question every cycle.

content:
**Lymph** — clear, yellowish fluid in **lymph vessels** and lymphatic organs (lymph nodes, spleen, tonsils). Part of the **immune system**.

**Formation:**
• Blood plasma filters out through capillary walls into tissue spaces → **tissue fluid**.
• Most tissue fluid re-enters capillaries; remainder enters **lymph vessels** → becomes lymph → eventually returns to blood via large lymph ducts (thoracic duct).

**Composition:**
• Similar to plasma but **no RBCs** (and fewer proteins).
• Contains **lymphocytes** and **monocytes** (WBC types).

**Functions of lymph:**
• **Drainage** — collects excess tissue fluid, metabolites and proteins; returns them to blood.
• **Absorption** — fats from digested food absorbed through **lacteals** (lymph vessels in intestinal villi).
• **Defence** — lymphocytes destroy germs; lymph nodes filter bacteria and foreign particles.

**Three principal body fluids:** Blood, Lymph, Tissue fluid.

**Must know**
• Lymph cannot carry O₂/CO₂ — no haemoglobin (no RBCs).
• Lymph is part of immune defence, not primary gas transport.
• Fat absorption in intestine is through lymph (lacteals), not blood capillaries.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Lymph — tissue fluid collected in lymph vessels; contains WBCs, no RBCs.
• Lacteals — lymph capillaries in villi of small intestine absorbing digested fats.
• Lymph node — small gland filtering lymph and trapping pathogens.

**Exam traps:**
• 'Oxygen is transported by lymph' is FALSE.
• 'Lymph = blood' is FALSE — different composition and function.
• Fatty food absorption → lymphatic system, not directly into blood.
teacherTip: Lymph = Leaks from capillaries + Lacks RBCs + Lymphocytes for defence.
examTip: Give three functions of lymph: drainage, fat absorption, defence — textbook exact answer for long questions.

content:
Human blood groups are determined by **antigens** (proteins) present on the surface of **RBCs**. Discovered by **Karl Landsteiner**.

**Four blood groups:**
• **Group A** — has **A antigen** on RBCs; plasma has **anti-B** antibody.
• **Group B** — has **B antigen**; plasma has **anti-A** antibody.
• **Group AB** — has **both A and B antigens**; **no antibodies** in plasma → **universal recipient** (can receive from A, B, AB, O).
• **Group O** — **no antigens** on RBCs; plasma has **both anti-A and anti-B** antibodies → **universal donor** (can donate to A, B, AB, O).

**Transfusion rule:**
• Donor RBC antigens must NOT be attacked by recipient's antibodies → **matching** is essential.
• Group O RBCs have no antigens → safe to give to any group (universal donor).
• Group AB has no antibodies → can receive any group (universal recipient).

**Must know**
• Universal donor = **O** (not AB).
• Universal recipient = **AB** (not O).
• Antigens are ON RBCs; antibodies are IN plasma.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Antigen — substance on RBC surface that triggers immune response (A or B antigen).
• Antibody — protein in plasma that reacts with foreign antigens (anti-A or anti-B).
• Universal donor — Group O (no A or B antigens on RBCs).

**Exam traps:**
• 'AB is universal donor' is FALSE — AB is universal recipient.
• 'O is universal recipient' is FALSE — O is universal donor.
• Mixing incompatible blood causes **agglutination** (clumping) — dangerous.
teacherTip: O gives to All (donor). AB receives from All (recipient).
examTip: Statement-based MCQs swap donor/recipient for AB and O — read carefully before ticking.

content:
The heart beats rhythmically due to its own **electrical conduction system** — specialised cardiac muscle fibres generate and conduct impulses.

**SA node (Sino-Atrial node):**
• Located in the wall of the **right atrium**.
• Called the **'pacemaker'** of the heart — initiates each heartbeat.
• Generates electrical impulses that spread through atria → AV node → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles contract.
• Sets normal resting heart rate (~72 beats/min).

**Sequence of contraction:**
• **Atria contract first** (atrial systole) → push blood into ventricles.
• **Ventricles contract next** (ventricular systole) → push blood into arteries.
• Brief pause (diastole) → heart relaxes and refills.

**Artificial pacemaker:**
• Electronic device surgically implanted when **SA node is damaged** or conduction system fails.
• Sends regular electrical signals to keep heart beating normally.

**Must know**
• SA node = natural pacemaker.
• Impulse starts in RA (SA node), not in ventricles.
• Heart is myogenic — beats on its own without brain signal (though nerves can speed/slow it).
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Pacemaker (SA node) — specialised tissue in right atrium initiating heartbeat electrical impulse.
• Artificial pacemaker — mechanical device replacing faulty SA node to maintain regular heartbeat.
• Systole — phase of heart contraction; Diastole — phase of relaxation.

**Exam traps:**
• SA node is in right atrium, NOT left ventricle.
• 'Brain directly makes heart beat' is oversimplified — SA node initiates; autonomic nerves modulate rate.
• Artificial pacemaker used when SA node fails, not for high blood pressure alone.
teacherTip: SA = Starts the Action. Right Atrium = Right place for the pacemaker.
examTip: Match: SA node → Pacemaker. Name (b) structure starting electrical impulses → SA node / sino-atrial node.

content:
**PULMONARY CIRCULATION** (lungs circuit — right side of heart):
• Path: **RA → RV → Pulmonary artery → Lungs** (gas exchange: CO₂ out, O₂ in) **→ Pulmonary veins → LA**.
• Blood becomes **oxygenated** in lungs (alveoli).
• Short, low-resistance circuit.
• Only circuit where artery carries deoxygenated blood and vein carries oxygenated blood.

**SYSTEMIC CIRCULATION** (body circuit — left side of heart):
• Path: **LA → LV → Aorta → Arteries → Capillaries** (O₂ delivered, CO₂ collected in tissues) **→ Veins → Vena cava → RA**.
• **Aorta** — largest artery; branches to head, limbs, organs.
• **Vena cava** (superior + inferior) — returns deoxygenated blood to RA.
• Long, high-pressure circuit.

**Gas exchange:**
• Lungs: blood O₂ increases, CO₂ decreases.
• Body tissues: blood O₂ decreases, CO₂ increases.

**Must know**
• Blood is oxygenated in **lungs** (not in heart).
• Left side of heart always has **oxygenated** blood.
• Right side of heart always has **deoxygenated** blood.
• Pulmonary circulation = to lungs; Systemic = to body.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Pulmonary circulation — circulation of blood between heart and lungs for oxygenation.
• Systemic circulation — circulation of blood between heart and rest of body for nutrient/gas exchange.
• Aorta — main artery carrying oxygenated blood from left ventricle to body.

**Exam traps:**
• Pulmonary artery path: RV → lungs (NOT LA → lungs).
• 'Oxygenation occurs in heart' is FALSE — occurs in lung alveoli.
• Match path MCQ: RA→RV→Pulmonary arteries→Lungs→Pulmonary veins→LA is correct pulmonary route.
teacherTip: Pulmonary = Puff fresh air (lungs). Systemic = Supply the body.
examTip: Draw/trace: Body→Right heart→Lungs→Left heart→Body — this loop diagram alone can earn 3–4 diagram marks.
